stem: Human growth hormone (HGH) has a precise molecular structure. Which description is MOST completely and correctly stated?
options: HGH is a 191-amino acid single-chain polypeptide containing two disulphide bridges; it is secreted by acidophils called somatotropes, the most abundant cells of the pituitary
HGH is a 192-amino acid single-chain polypeptide containing two disulphide bridges; it is secreted by acidophils called somatotropes, the most abundant cells of the pituitary
HGH is a 191-amino acid single-chain polypeptide containing three disulphide bridges; it is secreted by acidophils called somatotropes, the most abundant cells of the pituitary
HGH is a 191-amino acid double-chain polypeptide containing two disulphide bridges; it is secreted by basophils called somatotropes, the most abundant cells of the pituitary

stem: The normal human growth hormone is coded by a specific gene located at a specific chromosomal position. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Coded by the hGH-N gene located on the long arm (q22-24 region) of chromosome 17; this variant accounts for 75% of all growth hormones
Coded by the hGH-N gene located on the short arm (p22-24 region) of chromosome 17; this variant accounts for 75% of all growth hormones
Coded by the hGH-N gene located on the long arm (q22-24 region) of chromosome 7; this variant accounts for 75% of all growth hormones
Coded by the hGH-N gene located on the long arm (q22-24 region) of chromosome 17; this variant accounts for 80% of all growth hormones

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks about another variant form of GH that appears during pregnancy. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Another variant form of GH is produced by the placenta, which appears appreciably in the blood during pregnancy
Another variant form of GH is produced by the liver, which appears appreciably in the blood during pregnancy
Another variant form of GH is produced by the decidua, which appears appreciably in the blood during pregnancy
Another variant form of GH is produced by the fetal pituitary, which appears appreciably in the blood during pregnancy

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q3): The student asks about species specificity of GH. Which statement is MOST precisely derived from the notes?
options: There are considerable variations in the structure of GH from species to species; thus GH from one species may not be active when administered to another species
There are minimal variations in the structure of GH from species to species; thus GH from one species is generally active when administered to another species
There are no meaningful variations in the structure of GH from species to species; only immunological cross-reactivity varies across species
There are considerable variations in the function of GH across species; however, the molecular structure of GH is universally conserved

stem: GH secretion and metabolism involves a specific initial production step. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: GH is produced as a prohormone in the ribosomes and undergoes post-translational modification to make the active hormone; it is stored in secretory vesicles and released by exocytosis
GH is produced as a pre-prohormone in the rough endoplasmic reticulum and undergoes co-translational cleavage to make the active hormone; it is stored in secretory vesicles
GH is produced directly as an active hormone in the ribosomes without any post-translational modification; it is immediately secreted without storage in vesicles
GH is produced as a prohormone in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum and undergoes post-translational modification; it is stored in the Golgi apparatus

stem: GH has a specific half-life and daily output. Which values are MOST precisely stated?
options: GH has a 6-20 minute half-life and a daily output of 0.18-0.98 mg/day in adults
GH has a 6-20 minute half-life and a daily output of 1.8-9.8 mg/day in adults
GH has a 20-60 minute half-life and a daily output of 0.18-0.98 mg/day in adults
GH has a 6-20 minute half-life and a daily output of 0.018-0.098 mg/day in adults

stem: CASE STUDY: A student traces the baseline GH level in adults and children. Which statement is MOST precisely derived from the notes?
options: The baseline level is less than 5ng/ml in adults and 5ng/ml in children; indeed values are higher in early neonatal periods, fall during childhood, rise again at puberty, and fall during ageing

The baseline level is less than 5ng/ml in adults and greater than 5ng/ml in children; values are lower in early neonatal periods, rise during childhood, reach a peak at puberty, and fall during ageing
The baseline level is less than 1ng/ml in adults and less than 5ng/ml in children; values are constant throughout early neonatal periods and childhood then rise sharply at puberty
The baseline level is greater than 5ng/ml in adults and less than 5ng/ml in children; values are higher in adults because GH promotes tissue maintenance rather than growth

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q7): After secretion, GH is bound to plasma proteins. What percentage is loosely bound?
options: About 50% is loosely bound to plasma proteins (GH-binding protein); secretion occurs in a pulsatile manner and it is rapidly metabolised in the liver and excreted
About 25% is loosely bound to plasma proteins (GH-binding protein); secretion occurs in a pulsatile manner and it is rapidly metabolised in the kidney and excreted
About 75% is loosely bound to plasma proteins (GH-binding protein); secretion occurs in a pulsatile manner and it is rapidly metabolised in the liver and excreted
About 50% is tightly bound to plasma proteins (GH-binding protein); secretion occurs in a continuous manner and it is metabolised in the liver

stem: GH acts primarily by binding to receptors on the cell surface. The receptor belongs to a specific superfamily. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: The GH receptor is a 620-amino acid homodimer and a member of the cytokine receptor superfamily; like insulin, it activates many different intracellular enzyme cascades and enables the JAKs-STAT pathway
The GH receptor is a 620-amino acid heterodimer and a member of the cytokine receptor superfamily; like insulin, it activates many different intracellular enzyme cascades
The GH receptor is a 420-amino acid homodimer and a member of the tyrosine kinase receptor superfamily; like insulin, it activates many different intracellular enzyme cascades
The GH receptor is a 620-amino acid homodimer and a member of the G-protein coupled receptor superfamily; unlike insulin, it activates second messenger cascades only

stem: CASE STUDY: A student traces the GH signalling cascade from receptor binding. Which sequence is MOST precisely correct?
options: GH binds receptor â activates JAKS-2 (member of Janus family of cytoplasmic tyrosine kinases) â STAT means signal transducers and activators of transcription â binding of GH to receptors leads to dimerisation of receptors â activating Janus kinases â phosphorylates STAT proteins â transcription with subsequent translation of proteins necessary for GH action; also acts through MAP kinase pathways for chondrogenesis
GH binds receptor â activates G-protein â generates cAMP â activates protein kinase A â phosphorylates target enzymes â transcription of growth-related genes
GH binds receptor â autophosphorylates tyrosine residues on the receptor â activates PI-3 kinase â generates PIP3 â activates Akt â phosphorylates STAT proteins
GH binds receptor â activates JAKS-2 â phosphorylates tyrosine on the receptor â activates PLC â generates IP3 and DAG â activates protein kinase C

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q10): STAT is an acronym. What does STAT stand for?
options: Signal transducers and activators of transcription
Signal transference and amplifiers of translation
Steroid transcription and activators of translation
Signal transducers and amplifiers of transcription

stem: GH is also known by an alternative name. What is this name?
options: GH is also called somatotropic hormone
GH is also called somatostatin
GH is also called somatomedins
GH is also called somatocrinin

stem: CASE STUDY: A student describes the effect of GH on bone and cartilage. Which statement is MOST completely and precisely correct?
options: GH increases deposition of proteins by chondrogenic and osteogenic cells; stimulates new bone formation and converts chondrocytes into osteogenic cells; long bones grow in length in the epiphyseal cartilage with elongation of the shaft, pushing epiphyses further apart; epiphyseal cartilage is used progressively until fusion occurs between shaft and epiphyses; after this, growth in length stops but osteoblastic activity continues to thicken the jaw and skull bones; osteoclasts institute remodelling; epiphyses close at specific ages forming the basis for using X-rays to determine age

GH increases deposition of proteins by osteogenic cells only; stimulates osteoclasts to resorb bone and lay down new osteoid; long bones grow at the metaphysis rather than the epiphysis; fusion occurs at the epiphyseal plates; after fusion, growth continues through periosteal deposition exclusively
GH stimulates cartilage proliferation exclusively without affecting bone formation; it promotes chondrocyte hypertrophy but does not convert chondrocytes into osteogenic cells
GH increases deposition of proteins by chondrogenic cells; long bones grow by periosteal apposition rather than epiphyseal growth; osteoclasts remodel bone after epiphyseal fusion occurs

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q13): GH has specific effects on protein metabolism. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: GH increases amino acid transport into cells, stimulates protein synthesis, and inhibits protein catabolism; it is an anabolic protein hormone and produces a positive nitrogen balance
GH decreases amino acid transport into cells but stimulates protein synthesis and inhibits protein catabolism; it produces a positive nitrogen balance
GH increases amino acid transport into cells but inhibits protein synthesis while stimulating protein catabolism; it produces a negative nitrogen balance
GH increases amino acid transport into cells, stimulates protein synthesis, and stimulates protein catabolism equally; it has no net effect on nitrogen balance

stem: GH stimulates lipolysis through a specific mechanism and the resulting free fatty acids have a specific fate. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: GH stimulates lipolysis by mobilising fat from adipose tissue; the released free fatty acids (FFAs) are utilised for energy production; the resultant effect is a tendency towards ketogenesis; it provides an alternative energy source during fasting, hypoglycaemia, and stressful situations, conserving protein for growth and development
GH stimulates lipolysis by mobilising fat from the liver; the released FFAs are utilised for energy production; the resultant effect is a tendency towards gluconeogenesis; it conserves protein
GH stimulates lipogenesis by mobilising fat from adipose tissue; the released FFAs are stored as triglycerides; the resultant effect is a tendency toward obesity; it increases protein catabolism
GH stimulates lipolysis by mobilising fat from adipose tissue; the released FFAs inhibit glucose oxidation; the resultant effect is a tendency towards hypoglycaemia; it promotes protein catabolism

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse explains GH's effects on carbohydrate metabolism. Which is MOST completely correct?
options: GH reduces peripheral utilisation of glucose and decreases glucose uptake by muscle; enhances hepatic glucose output; during fasting GH inhibits glycogenolysis in skeletal and cardiac muscles (glycostatic effect); increases blood sugar; secondarily stimulates B cells of the pancreas to produce insulin; continuous sustained stimulation may burn out beta cells leading to diabetes mellitus (diabetogenic effect)

GH increases peripheral utilisation of glucose and increases glucose uptake by muscle; decreases hepatic glucose output; stimulates glycogenolysis in skeletal and cardiac muscles; decreases blood sugar; suppresses B cells of the pancreas

GH reduces peripheral utilisation of glucose and decreases glucose uptake by muscle; decreases hepatic glucose output; during fasting GH promotes glycogenolysis in skeletal muscles; decreases blood sugar; stimulates B cells to produce glucagon
GH reduces peripheral glucose utilisation; increases gluconeogenesis in the liver; inhibits insulin secretion directly; produces hyperglycaemia through direct suppression of peripheral glucose uptake

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q16): In electrolyte metabolism, GH increases absorption of which two minerals from the gastrointestinal and renal systems?
options: Calcium and phosphorous
Calcium and sodium
Phosphorous and potassium
Sodium and calcium

stem: GH reduces the excretion of two specific ions, conserving them for incorporation into growing cells. Which pair is MOST precisely stated?
options: Sodium (Na+) and potassium (K+)
Sodium (Na+) and chloride (Cl-)
Calcium (Ca2+) and phosphate (PO4-)
Potassium (K+) and calcium (Ca2+)

stem: CASE STUDY: A patient develops diabetes mellitus after years of acromegaly. Based on the notes, which mechanism explains this?
options: Continuous and sustained stimulation of B cells by GH-induced hyperglycaemia may burn out the beta cells, leading to diabetes mellitus â this is the diabetogenic effect of GH
GH directly destroys pancreatic beta cells through an immune mechanism, reducing insulin production
GH causes direct inhibition of insulin gene transcription, progressively reducing insulin synthesis over years
GH promotes glucagon secretion which competitively inhibits insulin binding to its receptors, producing functional insulin resistance

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q19): The physician explains that GH acts directly on tissues with specific effects. Which direct actions of GH are MOST precisely listed?
options: GH is believed to act directly to stimulate lipolysis, protein synthesis, epiphyseal growth, decreased insulin sensitivity, and sodium retention
GH is believed to act directly to stimulate lipolysis, protein synthesis, epiphyseal growth, increased insulin sensitivity, and potassium retention
GH is believed to act directly to stimulate lipogenesis, protein synthesis, epiphyseal growth, decreased insulin sensitivity, and sodium retention
GH is believed to act directly to stimulate lipolysis, protein catabolism, epiphyseal growth, decreased insulin sensitivity, and sodium excretion

stem: Somatomedins are polypeptides that belong to a large molecular family. Which description is MOST precisely correct?
options: Somatomedins are polypeptides belonging to a large family of growth factors; they are secreted mainly by the liver and to a small extent by other tissues like the kidneys and muscles; in humans only two have been identified to have physiological importance â IGF-1 (somatomedin C) the more abundant, and IGF-2 (the less abundant)

Somatomedins are glycoproteins belonging to a large family of growth factors; they are secreted mainly by the liver and to a small extent by the kidneys; in humans only three have been identified to have physiological importance including IGF-1, IGF-2, and IGF-3

Somatomedins are polypeptides belonging to a large family of growth factors; they are secreted exclusively by the liver; in humans only one has been identified to have physiological importance â IGF-1 (somatomedin C)

Somatomedins are polypeptides belonging to a small family of growth factors; they are secreted mainly by the kidneys and to a small extent by the liver; in humans only two have been identified â IGF-1 (somatomedin A) and IGF-2

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks specifically about IGF-1 and its relationship to GH control. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: IGF-1 is NOT under the control of GH in utero; however, GH begins to influence its production immediately postpartum, with plasma levels rising through childhood, reaching a zenith at puberty, and gradually falling with old age
IGF-1 IS under the control of GH both in utero and postpartum; plasma levels rise through childhood, reaching a zenith at puberty, and gradually falling with old age
IGF-1 is NOT under the control of GH in utero; plasma levels remain constant throughout life because IGF-1 is constitutively produced by the liver
IGF-1 is under the control of GH only during puberty; before puberty and after puberty, IGF-1 is produced independently of GH

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q22): What is the specific role of IGF-2?
options: IGF-2 likely has a responsibility in utero of assisting foetal growth; plasma levels are constant throughout postnatal life
IGF-2 is responsible for postnatal linear growth and IGF-1 is responsible for foetal growth â they have opposite developmental roles
IGF-2 is the more abundant of the two IGFs and has a responsibility postpartum for promoting linear bone growth
IGF-2 has no established role in utero and functions exclusively as a mitogen in postnatal life

stem: Somatomedins bind to plasma proteins in a specific manner. Which statement is MOST precisely correct?
options: Most somatomedins are tightly bound to plasma proteins, more significantly to IGF binding protein 3; this ensures a prolongation of the half-life to about 20 hours, in contrast to GH which is loosely bound to proteins with a short half-life

Most somatomedins are loosely bound to plasma proteins, specifically to IGF binding protein 3; this ensures a short half-life of about 2 hours, in contrast to GH which is tightly bound with a long half-life
Most somatomedins are tightly bound to plasma proteins, more significantly to IGF binding protein 1; this ensures a prolongation of the half-life to about 20 hours

Most somatomedins are tightly bound to plasma proteins, more significantly to IGF binding protein 3; this ensures a prolongation of the half-life to about 2 hours, in contrast to GH which has a half-life of 20 hours

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks why the biological importance of somatomedins is more prolonged than GH. Which is MOST precisely derived from the notes?
options: Because somatomedins are tightly bound to plasma proteins with a half-life of about 20 hours, while GH is loosely bound with a short half-life; the physiological importance therefore is that the biological activity of somatomedins is more prolonged than GH
Because somatomedins are produced continuously without pulsatile secretion, while GH is secreted in pulses with intervening low levels
Because somatomedins are smaller molecules that diffuse more readily into tissues, prolonging their tissue exposure compared to larger GH molecules
Because somatomedins are sequestered in the extracellular matrix and released slowly, prolonging their biological availability compared to freely circulating GH

stem: Somatomedins are hydrophilic. What does this mean for their mechanism of action?
options: Because somatomedins are hydrophilic, they attach to cell membrane receptors in their target cells; the receptors resemble insulin receptors; the ligand-receptor complex leads to autophosphorylation and modification of intracellular enzymes
Because somatomedins are hydrophilic, they diffuse directly across cell membranes to bind intracellular receptors similar to steroid hormones
Because somatomedins are hydrophilic, they activate G-protein coupled receptors to generate cAMP as a second messenger
Because somatomedins are hydrophilic, they cannot bind to receptors and instead act through ligand-independent receptor activation

stem: CASE STUDY: The interaction between GH and somatomedins is described. Which statement is MOST precisely correct?
options: The interaction is variable; it is generally believed that GH stimulates the liver to produce somatomedins which act on bone, cartilage, and other tissues; recent evidence shows GH stimulates hepatic synthesis of IGF-1 which stimulates epiphyseal growth and possesses insulin-like activity; it enhances protein synthesis and inhibits lipolysis
The interaction is direct; GH acts entirely through somatomedins with no direct tissue effects of its own; somatomedins mediate 100% of GH's anabolic effects
The interaction is simple; GH directly stimulates all tissues without involving somatomedins; somatomedins are produced constitutively independent of GH and serve as tissue growth factors
The interaction is well established; GH acts only on the liver to produce IGF-1; IGF-1 mediates all metabolic effects of GH; GH itself has no direct biological actions on peripheral tissues

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q27): IGF-1 stimulated by GH from the liver specifically possesses what activity?
options: IGF-1 stimulates epiphyseal growth and possesses insulin-like activity; it enhances protein synthesis and inhibits lipolysis
IGF-1 stimulates epiphyseal growth and possesses anti-insulin activity; it inhibits protein synthesis and promotes lipolysis
IGF-1 stimulates epiphyseal growth and possesses glucagon-like activity; it promotes glycogenolysis and inhibits protein synthesis
IGF-1 stimulates epiphyseal growth and possesses purely anabolic activity without any insulin-like metabolic effects

stem: GH stimulates erythropoiesis. Which ions does GH conserve to support growing cells and tissues?
options: GH reduces excretion of sodium (Na+) and potassium (K+); conservation makes them available for incorporation into growing cells and tissues
GH reduces excretion of calcium (Ca2+) and phosphate (PO4-); conservation makes them available for incorporation into growing cells and tissues
GH reduces excretion of sodium (Na+) and chloride (Cl-); conservation makes them available for incorporation into growing cells and tissues
GH reduces excretion of potassium (K+) and calcium (Ca2+); conservation makes them available for incorporation into growing cells and tissues

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is explaining why the glycostatic effect of GH is paradoxical. Which explanation is MOST grounded in the notes?
options: During fasting, GH inhibits glycogenolysis in skeletal and cardiac muscles â the glycostatic effect â which paradoxically tends to increase blood sugar because muscle glucose uptake is simultaneously reduced, creating a net hyperglycaemia
During fasting, GH promotes glycogenolysis in skeletal muscles â releasing glucose â which paradoxically decreases blood sugar because the released glucose is immediately oxidised by muscle
During fasting, GH stimulates glycogen synthesis in cardiac muscle â the glycostatic effect â which paradoxically depletes liver glycogen reserves and produces hypoglycaemia
During fasting, GH inhibits hepatic glycogen breakdown â the glycostatic effect â which directly reduces blood glucose levels and prevents hyperglycaemia

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q30): The student asks what specific cells in the epiphysis GH acts on for bone growth. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: GH acts on chondrogenic cells in the epiphyseal cartilage; it increases deposition of proteins by chondrogenic and osteogenic cells; it stimulates new bone formation and converts chondrocytes into osteogenic cells
GH acts exclusively on osteoblasts in the periosteum; chondrocytes are not directly stimulated by GH
GH acts on osteoclasts in the epiphyseal cartilage; it promotes bone resorption and remodelling without directly stimulating bone formation
GH acts on fibroblasts in the epiphyseal growth plate; fibroblasts are converted to chondrocytes which then produce cartilage matrix

stem: GH acts via the MAP kinase pathway for a specific process. What is this process?
options: GH acts through the mitogen-activated protein (MAP) kinase pathway for chondrogenesis
GH acts through the mitogen-activated protein (MAP) kinase pathway for lipolysis
GH acts through the mitogen-activated protein (MAP) kinase pathway for protein synthesis
GH acts through the mitogen-activated protein (MAP) kinase pathway for erythropoiesis

stem: CASE STUDY: A student confuses the role of osteoblasts and osteoclasts in GH-mediated bone remodelling. Which is MOST precisely correct from the notes?
options: After growth in length stops following epiphyseal fusion, osteoblastic activity continues to thicken the jaw and skull bones (deposition of new bones on the surface of old ones); osteoclasts then institute remodelling
After growth in length stops following epiphyseal fusion, osteoclastic activity thickens the jaw and skull bones; osteoblasts then institute remodelling to smooth the surface
After growth in length stops, both osteoblasts and osteoclasts simultaneously deposit and resorb bone in equal amounts, maintaining bone mass without net change
After growth in length stops, only osteoclasts are active; osteoblasts are dormant until a fracture signal activates them for repair

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q33): Epiphyses close at specific ages. Why is this clinically significant?
options: Various epiphyses close at specific ages in average persons, which forms the basis for using X-rays to determine the age of individuals
Various epiphyses close at specific ages, which forms the basis for diagnosing GH deficiency by measuring bone density
Various epiphyses close at specific ages, which forms the basis for prescribing GH replacement therapy only before puberty
Various epiphyses close at specific ages, which forms the basis for monitoring GH therapy response by measuring growth velocity

stem: GH stimulates cellular multiplication. What specific effect does it have at the cellular level?
options: GH stimulates the growth of almost all tissues of the body that can grow; at the cellular level it enhances mitosis and increases the size of cells
GH stimulates the growth of only bone and cartilage; at the cellular level it enhances protein synthesis without affecting cell division
GH stimulates the growth of almost all tissues of the body; at the cellular level it inhibits apoptosis and increases the size of cells without affecting mitosis
GH stimulates the growth of muscle and bone only; at the cellular level it enhances hypertrophy but not hyperplasia

stem: CASE STUDY: A student lists the evidence showing controversy about GH vs IGF-1 physiological activity. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: There was controversy about whether the physiological activity was peculiar to GH or IGF-1; however, recent evidence shows GH stimulates hepatic synthesis of IGF-1, which stimulates epiphyseal growth and possesses insulin-like activity
There was no controversy; it has always been established that GH acts exclusively through IGF-1 as its sole mediator
There was controversy resolved in favour of IGF-1; GH itself has no direct physiological actions and all effects are mediated through IGF-1
There was controversy resolved in favour of GH; IGF-1 has no independent physiological actions beyond serving as a marker of GH activity

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q36): The somatomedin receptor resembles a specific other receptor, leading to a specific signalling consequence. Which is MOST correctly stated?
options: The somatomedin receptors resemble insulin receptors; therefore, the ligand-receptor complex leads to autophosphorylation and modification of intracellular enzymes
The somatomedin receptors resemble GH receptors; therefore, the ligand-receptor complex activates JAK-STAT pathways
The somatomedin receptors resemble glucagon receptors; therefore, the ligand-receptor complex generates cAMP via G-protein activation
The somatomedin receptors resemble steroid hormone receptors; therefore, the ligand-receptor complex translocates to the nucleus to activate gene transcription directly

stem: The GH receptor activates JAK-S2. What is JAK-S2 classified as?
options: JAKS-2 is a member of the Janus family of cytoplasmic tyrosine kinases
JAKS-2 is a member of the Janus family of nuclear serine kinases
JAKS-2 is a member of the MAP kinase family of cytoplasmic tyrosine kinases
JAKS-2 is a member of the src family of membrane-bound tyrosine kinases

stem: CASE STUDY: A patient with Laron syndrome has high GH but very low IGF-1. Based on the notes, which mechanism is MOST directly impaired?
options: GH cannot stimulate the liver to produce somatomedins because GH receptors in the liver are defective; therefore, hepatic IGF-1 synthesis is absent despite high GH levels
GH receptors are absent in the pituitary, so GH production is unregulated and liver IGF-1 production is secondarily suppressed
GH is structurally abnormal and binds liver receptors but cannot activate the JAK-STAT pathway needed for IGF-1 gene transcription
IGF-1 is degraded rapidly by elevated IGF binding protein-3 that is paradoxically increased by excess GH signalling

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q39): The clinician notes this patient has tall parents and should have normal stature, but is short. The binding of GH to its receptor leads to a specific molecular event. What is this?
options: Binding of GH to its receptors leads to dimerisation of the receptors, activating Janus kinases, which in turn phosphorylates STAT proteins
Binding of GH to its receptors leads to autophosphorylation of tyrosine residues on the receptor itself, directly activating MAP kinase
Binding of GH to its receptors leads to G-protein activation, which generates cAMP as a second messenger to activate protein kinase A
Binding of GH to its receptors leads to receptor internalisation, which carries the signal to the nucleus for direct gene activation

stem: The chromosome location of the hGH-N gene is described precisely. Which gene arm and band is MOST correct?
options: Long arm (q22-24 region) of chromosome 17
Short arm (p22-24 region) of chromosome 17
Long arm (q22-24 region) of chromosome 7
Long arm (q22-24 region) of chromosome 11

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks what the specific IGF binding protein that prolongs somatomedin half-life is called. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: IGF binding protein 3 (IGFBP-3) is the most significant plasma binding protein for somatomedins
IGF binding protein 1 (IGFBP-1) is the most significant plasma binding protein for somatomedins
IGF binding protein 5 (IGFBP-5) is the most significant plasma binding protein for somatomedins
IGF binding protein 2 (IGFBP-2) is the most significant plasma binding protein for somatomedins

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q42): The student asks specifically how long the somatomedin half-life is due to binding protein protection. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: About 20 hours, in contrast to GH which is loosely bound to proteins with a short half-life
About 2 hours, in contrast to GH which is loosely bound to proteins with a short half-life
About 20 days, in contrast to GH which has a half-life of hours
About 20 minutes, which is the same half-life as GH

stem: The hGH-N gene variant accounts for what percentage of all growth hormones?
options: 75% of all growth hormones
80% of all growth hormones
50% of all growth hormones
90% of all growth hormones

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks about GH secretion pattern. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Secretion occurs in a pulsatile manner; it is rapidly metabolised in the liver and excreted
Secretion occurs in a continuous tonic manner; it is rapidly metabolised in the kidney and excreted
Secretion occurs in a circadian pattern with a single daily peak; it is metabolised in the liver
Secretion occurs episodically with secretory bursts only during sleep; it is metabolised in the kidney

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q45): A student confuses the IGF-1 and IGF-2 relationship with GH. Which statement MOST precisely distinguishes their GH control?
options: IGF-1 is NOT under GH control in utero but comes under GH control immediately postpartum; IGF-2 likely assists foetal growth in utero with plasma levels constant throughout postnatal life
Both IGF-1 and IGF-2 are under GH control in utero and postpartum; only their respective tissue targets differ
IGF-1 is under GH control both in utero and postpartum; IGF-2 has no relationship with GH at any stage of life
IGF-2 is under GH control in utero but not postpartum; IGF-1 comes under GH control only at puberty

stem: GH stimulates erythropoiesis as one of its physiological actions. This is listed as action number:
options: iv. GH stimulates erythropoiesis â listed as the fourth physiological action after cellular multiplication, bone and cartilage effects, and metabolic actions
i. GH stimulates erythropoiesis â listed as the first physiological action
ii. GH stimulates erythropoiesis â listed as the second physiological action after cellular multiplication
v. GH stimulates erythropoiesis â listed as the fifth physiological action

stem: Human growth hormone (HGH) is a _______________ amino acid single-chain polypeptide containing two disulphide bridges.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'The hGH-N gene is located on the long arm (q22-24 region) of chromosome _______________.' One number must be supplied.
The hGH-N gene variant accounts for _______________% of all growth hormones.
CASE STUDY: An exam states: 'GH has a _______________ to 20 minute half-life and a daily output of 0.18-0.98 mg/day in adults.' One number must be supplied.
About _______________% of GH is loosely bound to plasma proteins (GH-binding protein); it is secreted in a pulsatile manner and rapidly metabolised in the liver.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'The GH receptor is a 620-amino acid _______________ and a member of the cytokine receptor superfamily.' One structural term must be supplied.
STAT stands for signal transducers and _______________ of transcription.
CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'GH acts through the mitogen-activated protein (MAP) kinase pathway for _______________.' One precise biological process must be supplied.
In protein metabolism, GH increases amino acid transport into cells, stimulates protein synthesis, and inhibits protein catabolism; thus it is an _______________ protein hormone.
CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'During fasting, GH inhibits glycogenolysis in skeletal and cardiac muscles; this is called the _______________ effect.' One precise term must be supplied.
Continuous and sustained stimulation of B cells of the pancreas by GH may burn out the beta cells, leading to diabetes mellitus; this is called the _______________ effect.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'In electrolyte metabolism, GH increases absorption of calcium and _______________ from the gastrointestinal and renal systems.' One mineral must be supplied.
Somatomedins are polypeptides and belong to a large family of growth factors; they are secreted mainly by the _______________ and to a small extent by other tissues like the kidneys and muscles.
CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'IGF-1 is NOT under the control of GH _______________.' One precise Latin term must be supplied.
IGF-1 plasma levels rise through childhood, reaching a _______________ at puberty, and gradually falling with old age.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'Most somatomedins are tightly bound to plasma proteins, more significantly to IGF binding protein _______________.' One number must be supplied.
IGF binding protein 3 ensures a prolongation of the somatomedin half-life to about _______________ hours, in contrast to GH which is loosely bound with a short half-life.
CASE STUDY: An exam states: 'Because somatomedins are _______________, they attach to cell membrane receptors in their target cells.' One precise solubility characteristic must be supplied.
The somatomedin receptors resemble _______________ receptors; therefore, the ligand-receptor complex leads to autophosphorylation and modification of intracellular enzymes.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'In humans, only two somatomedins have been identified to have physiological importance: IGF-1 (somatomedin _______________) and IGF-2.' One letter must be supplied.
GH stimulates lipolysis by mobilising fat from adipose tissue; the released free fatty acids are utilised for energy production; the resultant effect is a tendency towards _______________.

CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'GH is produced as a _______________ in the ribosomes and undergoes post-translational modification to make the active hormone.' One precise biochemical term must be supplied.
GH reduces the renal excretion of sodium (Na+) and _______________ ions; the conservation of these ions makes them readily available for incorporation into growing cells and tissues.
